coredata.dc:title: Comparative study on the heat generation behavior of lithium-ion batteries with different cathode materials using accelerating rate calorimetry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: Proceedings of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Chunjing
Wang, Fang
Fan, Bin
Ren, Shan
Zhang, Yuemeng
Han, Liqiong
Liu, Shiqiang
Xu, Sichuan
coredata.dc:description: Heat generation behavior is of great significance for the design of both battery cell and thermal management system. In the available literature, there is no report comparing thermal behaviors among different types of lithium ion batteries. In this work, heat generation power of the LFP-C and NCM-C lithium ion battery cells are evaluated using an extended-volume accelerating rate calorimetry. From the map of battery heat generation power, it is found that for the lithium ion battery with certain materials and formulas, there exists an obvious heat generation boundary which is in a power function with the temperature. For the comparison between these two different batteries, experimental results show that the variation tendency of heat generation power between the NCM-C and LFP-C batteries are totally different. In addition, the battery with a NCM cathode exceeds that with a LiFePO4 cathode in specific volume heat generation power.
coredata.dcterms:subject: lithium ion battery
cathode material
heat generation behavior
accelerated rate adiabatic calorimetry

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at w.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling Assessing the feasibility of using the heat demand-outdoor temperature function for a long-term district heat demand forecast I. AndriÄ‡a,b,c*, A. Pinaa, P. FerrÃ£oa, J. Fournierb., B. LacarriÃ¨rec, O. Le Correc aIN+ Center for Innovation, Technology and Policy Research - Instituto Superior TÃ©cnico, Av. Rovisco Pais 1, 1049-001 Lisbon, Portugal bVeolia Recherche & Innovation, 291 Avenue Dreyfous Daniel, 78520 Limay, France cDÃ©partement SystÃ¨mes Ã‰nergÃ©tiques et Environnement - IMT Atlantique, 4 rue Alfred Kastler, 44300 Nantes, France Abstract District heating networks are commonly addressed in the literature as one of the most effective solutions for decreasing the greenhouse gas emissions from the building sector. These systems require high investments which are returned through the heat sales. Due to the changed climate conditions and building renovation policies, heat demand in the future could decrease, prolonging the investment return period. The main scope of this paper is to assess the feasibility of using the heat demand â€“ outdoor temperature function for heat demand forecast. The district of Alvalade, located in Lisbon (Portugal), was used as a case study. The district is consisted of 665 buildings that vary in both construction period and typology. Three weather scenarios (low, medium, high) and three district renovation scenarios were developed (shallow, intermediate, deep). To estimate the error, obtained heat demand values were compared with results from a dynamic heat demand model, previously developed and validated by the authors. The results showed that when only weather change is considered, the margin of error could be acceptable for some applications (the error in annual demand was lower than 20% for all weather scenarios considered). However, after introducing renovation scenarios, the error value increased up to 59.5% (depending on the weather and renovation scenarios combination considered). The value of slope coefficient increased on average within the range of 3.8% up to 8% per decade, that corresponds to the decrease in the number of heating hours of 22-139h during the heating season (depending on the combination of weather and renovation scenarios considered). On the other hand, function intercept increased for 7.8-12.7% per decade (depending on the coupled scenarios). The values suggested could be used to modify the function parameters for the scenarios considered, and improve the accuracy of heat demand estimations. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. Keywords: Heat demand; Forecast; Climate change Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 3369 3374 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy. 10.1016/j.egypro.2017.12.472 10.1016/j.egypro.2017.12.472 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Pu lished by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-rev ew under re ponsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conf rence on Applied Energy. Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ä£•ä£¥ä£«ä£§ä£°ä£¥ä£§ä£†ä£«ä£´ä£§ä£¥ä£¶ä¢¢ Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy. 9th International Conference on Applied Energy, ICAE2017, 21-24 August 2017, Cardiff, UK Comparative study on the heat generation behavior of lithium-ion batteries with different cathode materials using accelerating rate calorimetry Chunjing Lina, Fang Wanga*, Bin Fana, Shan Rena, Yuemeng Zhangb, Liqiong Hana, Shiqiang Liua, Sichuan Xub a China Automotive Technology and Research Center, 68 Xianfeng Road, Tianjin 300300, P.R.China bTongji University, 4800 Caoâ€™an Road, Shanghai, 201804, P.R.China Abstract Heat generation behavior is of great significance for the design of both battery cell and thermal management system. In the available literature, there is no report comparing thermal behaviors among different types of lithium ion batteries. In this work, heat generation power of the LFP-C and NCM-C lithium ion battery cells are evaluated using an extended-volume accelerating rate calorimetry. From the map of battery heat generation power, it is found that for the lithium ion battery with certain materials and formulas, there exists an obvious heat generation boundary which is in a power function with the temperature. For the comparison between these two different batteries, experimental results show that the variation tendency of heat generation power between the NCM-C and LFP-C batteries are totally different. In addition, the battery with a NCM cathode exceeds that with a LiFePO4 cathode in specific volume heat generation power. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. er-review u der responsibility of th scientific committe of the 9th I ternational Co fer nce on Applied Energy. Keywords: lithium ion battery; cathode material; heat generation behavior; accelerated rate adiabatic calorimetry 1. Introduction Due t the fav rable features of higher energy efficie cy and lower (or n ) emission to the environment, hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs), battery el ctric vehicl s (BEVs) and fuel cell vehicles ar regards as the best ca d ates for * Corresponding author. Tel.: +0-868-437-9666. E-mail address: wangfang2011@catarc.ac.cn 3370 Chunjing Lin et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 3369â€“3374 2 Chunjing Lin et al. / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 the conventional internal combustion vehicles [1,2]. With its high energy density, long cycle life, low self-discharge rate and other advantageous features, lithium ion battery is the primary choice for HEVs and BEVs [3-5]. However, lithium ion batteries are highly sensitive to the operating temperature. For example, low operating temperature will diminish the usable capacity and output power, and high temperature will accelerate the degradation rate of lithium ion batteries. Thus, it is important to investigate the thermal behavior of these batteries. For the better and much deeper understanding of the battery heat generation behavior, accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) is a useful tool. In previous works, Chen et al. [6] measured the heat generation power (HGP) of a 20 Ah LiFePO4 battery during discharges at current rates from 0.25C to 3C and initial temperatures from âˆ’10 to 40 Â°C. A LiFePO4 prismatic battery cell with a capacity of 40 Ah was analyzed also using calorimetry by Lin et al. [7] E. Schuster et al. [8] reported calorimetric data on the heat generation of a commercial 40 Ah LiNiCoMnO2 battery cell at various current rates. In addition, Veth et al. [9] investigated a pouch type Li-ion cell of 50 Ah during high current rate discharges. Such quantitative data are plentiful in literature. However, none of these works deals with the comparison between lithium ion batteries with different material systems. In this work, 40 Ah LFP-C and 36 Ah NCM-C lithium ion battery cells are evaluated using ARC. The HGP map and the idea of heat generation edge are proposed for the first time. Comparisons on the thermal behavior of these two battery cells are also given using the specific mass and specific volume HGPs. This work is in the hope of enlightening and deepening the understanding of heat generation behavior of lithium ion batteries in a new perspective. Nomenclature ARC accelerating rate calorimetry BEV battery electric vehicle EV+-ARC extended volume-accelerating rate calorimetry HEV hybrid electric vehicle HGP heat generation power LFP LiFePO4 NCM LiNiCoMnO2 2. Experimental 1.1. Battery cell The two types of battery cells used in this work belongs to the LiFePO4-C and LiNiCoMnO2-C families, respectively. The LiFePO4 cell has a capacity of 40Ah, mass of 1.138 kg and capacity of 1.066 kJ kgâˆ’1 Kâˆ’1. Other parameters are provided in referee [7].The NCM cell is 36 Ah in capacity, 0.692 kg in mass and 0.95953 kJ kgâˆ’1 Kâˆ’1 in specific capacity which is measured using the calorimetry. It is worth to mention that mass of the battery used for calculating the mass specific HGP is decided by calculating the average of 30 cells from the same bench. And the battery volume excludes the positive and negative tabs. 1.2. Experimental setup and process The electric-calorimetry coupled testing setup is shown in Fig. 1. Specifically, the battery heat generation is measured using an extended volume-accelerating rate calorimetry (EV+-ARC), produced by the Thermal Hazard TechnologyÂ®. The chamber in the calorimeter is 40 mm in diameter and 44 mm in depth, which can place most of the commercial lithium ion power batteries. Measuring temperature range of the ARC is from âˆ’50 to 400 Â°C with a resolution of 0.001 Â°C. Information of the battery charge and discharge facility is as follows: working voltage is Chunjing Lin et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 3369â€“3374 3371 Chunjing Lin et al. / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 3 between 0.1 V to 20 V, working current is between 0.5 A to 100 A, and resolutions of these above parameters are both Â±0.1% FS. Fig. 1. Schematic diagram of the electric-calorimetry coupled testing platform During the experiments, ARC can keep surface temperature of the chamber in accordance with the battery temperature instantaneously by applying the â€˜heat-wait-seekâ€™ mode. An adiabatic environment is thus created for the battery cell. With the measured capacity and temperature of the battery, heat generation power can be calculated by Equation 1. p= (d d )q mC T / t (1) in which q is HGP of a battery cell, kW; m is mass of the battery, kg; Cp is the specific heat capacity of the battery cell, kJ kgâˆ’1 Kâˆ’1; and T is the battery temperature, Â°C. The two different battery cells are evaluated at various initial temperatures and current rates. To create a low initial temperature, the ARC chamber was injected into liquid N2 in controlled quantity and the battery cell was placed in the chamber until thermal equilibrium was achieved before the beginning of each test. Charge profile of the battery is as follows: the battery is firstly charged at 0.33C until reached certain voltage and then kept at this voltage until the current decreases to 0.0165C. Discharge profile is as follows: the battery cell is discharged at a certain constant current rate until the voltage drops to the minimum voltage defined by the manufacture. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Heat generation rate map Heat generation of the LFP-C battery cell at various initial temperatures and current rates are shown in Fig. 2. As depicted in Fig. 2(a), when the initial temperature is high (i.e. higher than 20 Â°C), the heat generation power curve is much flat at the beginning period and rise sharply at the end of the discharge process. While when the initial temperature is as low as -12.5 â„ƒ,the beginning battery heat generation is very high due to a big internal resistance. With the continuing of the discharge process, the HGP decreases and finally rises again at the end period. In general, it can be observed from Fig. 2(a) that HGP during 1C-rate discharge can be divided into several regions according to the battery temperature. For example, between 0~25 Â°C and 25~45 Â°C, HGP is in the range of 7.5~15 W and 5~15 W, respectively. Thus, with such a map as shown in Figure 2(a), heat generation level at a certain current rate can be estimated. It is also very interesting that for a battery cell with certain materials and formulas, there exists a so called HGP boundary which is in a power function with the temperature as depicted by the black dot line in Figure 2(a). One of the goals of thermal design of the battery cell is to move down the HGP boundary in the vertical direction, which is helpful to decrease the thermal load and complexity of the battery thermal management system. Influence of the current rate on HGP is shown in Figure 2(b). It is evident that with the rising of current rate, heat generation level also augments. However, it should also be point that HGP surges in both magnitude and volatility 3372 Chunjing Lin et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 3369â€“3374 4 Chunjing Lin et al. / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 when the current rate increases from 1C to 2C. That is to say, thermal management system for lithium ion batteries meets with the challenges of both higher thermal load and quicker response sensitivity at higher current rates. Fig. 2. The heat generation power map of LFP-C battery cell at various operating conditions: (a) different initial temperatures at the same current rate; (b) different current rates at the same initial temperature. Heat generation powers of the NCM-C battery cell are plotted in Figure 3. As shown in Figure 3(a), the variation tendency of HGP curves of this NCM battery is quite different from those of the LiFePO4 cell, which means that SOC has more effect on the thermal behaviour of the NCM-C one. Specifically, when the initial battery temperature is much high, the HGP curve shows three peaks of waves, which happen at the beginning, near the end and at the end periods. While when the initial battery temperature is below zero, the characteristic of three crests disappears, while the HGP at the beginning of discharge process is as high as 3 times of that at the end period. Heat generation behaviour during 2C-rate discharge process shows exactly the same tendency as that during 1C-rate, but differs in magnitude, as shown in Figure 3(b). Fig. 3. The heat generation map of the NCM-C battery cell at various operating conditions: (a) different initial temperatures at the same current rate; (b) different current rates at the same initial temperature. Chunjing Lin et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 3369â€“3374 3373 Chunjing Lin et al. / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 5 3.2. Comparison on HGP of the LFP-C and NCM/C battery cells Fig. 4. Comparison on the heat generation power of the LFP-C and NCM-C battery cells at an initial temperature of 25 â„ƒ: (a) specific mass HGP during 1C-rate discharge; (b) specific volume HGP during 1C-rate dischargeï¼›(c) specific mass HGP during 2C-rate dischargeï¼›(d) specific volume HGP during 2C-rate discharge. The technology of lithium ion battery is changing quickly in these years. Cathode materials for lithium ion battery with new formulas are continually reported in literature and commercialized in the market. New batteries with much lower power generation level would decrease the chance of thermal runaway and require much simpler thermal management system. Using concepts of specific mass and specific volume HGP, heat generation magnitude of different batteries can be compared. Figure 4 shows the comparison on HGP of the LFP-C and NCM-C battery cells at various operating conditions. It can be observed that these two batteries are close to each other in the specific mass HGP during 1C-rate discharge, while the NCM-C battery exceeds in the specific volume HGP during the same course. During the 2C-rate discharge, the HGP of the NCM-C battery is higher than that of the LFP-C battery both in specific mass and specific volume HGP, and the difference in the specific volume HGP is much bigger. Thus, from Equation 2, it is evident that the NCM-C battery would go through a higher temperature rise at the same operating condition. d ( ) ( ( ) ( )) dp 0 q T t hC T t T t V t d ï�²ï€½ ï€« ï€­ (2) in which V is the volume of the battery, m3; Ï�is the density of the battery, kg mâˆ’3; t is time, s; T(t) is the battery temperature, K; h is the convective heat transfer coefficient, W mâˆ’2 Kâˆ’1; d is the thickness of the battery, m; T0(t) is the environment temperature, K. 3374 Chunjing Lin et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 3369â€“3374 6 Chunjing Lin et al. / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 4. Conclusion In this work, thermal behavior of the LFP-C and NCM-C lithium ion battery cells are evaluated using an EV+- ARC. From the HGP map, it is found that for lithium ion battery with certain materials and formulas, there exists a HGP boundary which is in a power function with the temperature. To the best of authorsâ€™ knowledge, the HGP map and edge are proposed for the first time in literature. For the comparison between different types of batteries, experimental results show that the variation tendencies of HGP curves between the NCM-C and LFP-C batteries are totally different. In addition, the battery with a NCM cathode exceeds the battery with a LiFePO4 cathode in the specific volume HGP. Acknowledgements This work is financially supported the National Key R&D Program of China (2016YFB0100400). References [1] Jarrett A, Kim IY. Design optimization of electric vehicle battery cooling plates for thermal performance. J Power Sources 2011;196:10359- 68. [2] Selman JR, Hallaj SA, Uchida I, Hirano Y. Cooperative research on safety fundamentals of lithium batteries. J Power Sources 2001;97â€“ 98:726-32. [3] Huang CK, Sakamoto JS, Wolfenstine J, Surampudi S. The Limits of Low-Temperature Performance of Li-Ion Cells. J Electrochemical Society 2000;147:2893-6 [4] Yuan T, Yu X, Cai R, Zhou YK, Shao ZP. Synthesis of pristine and carbon-coated Li4Ti5O12 and their low-temperature electrochemical performance. J Power Sources 2010;195:4997-5004. [5] Amine K, Chen CH, Liu J. Factors responsible for impedance rise in high power lithium ion batteries. J Power Sources 2001;97â€“98:684-7. [6] Chen K, Unsworth G, Li X. Measurements of heat generation in prismatic Li-ion batteries. J Power Sources 2014; 261:28-37. [7] Lin C, Xu S, Li Z, et al. Thermal analysis of large-capacity LiFePO 4, power batteries for electric vehicles[J]. Journal of Power Sources, 2015, 294:633-642. [8] Schuster E, Ziebert C, Melcher A, Rohde M, Seifert HJ. Thermal behavior and electrochemical heat generation in a commercial 40Ah lithium ion pouch cell. J Power Sources 2015;286:580-9. [9] Veth C, Dragicevic D, Merten C. Thermal characterizations of a large-format lithium ion cell focused on high current discharges. J Power Sources 2014;267:760-9. mass HGP during 2C-rate dischargeï¼›(d) specific volume HGP during 2C-rate discharge. The technology of lithium ion battery is changing quickly in these years. Cathode materials for lithium ion battery with new formulas are continually reported in literature and commercialized in the market. New batteries with much lower power generation level would decrease the chance of thermal runaway and require much simpler thermal management system. Using concepts of specific mass and specific volume HGP, heat generation magnitude of different batteries can be compared. Figure 4 shows the comparison on HGP of the LFP-C and NCM-C battery cells at various operating conditions. It can be observed that these two batteries are close to each other in the specific mass HGP during 1C-rate discharge, while the NCM-C battery exceeds in the specific volume HGP during the same course. During the 2C-rate discharge, the HGP of the NCM-C battery is higher than that of the LFP-C battery both in specific mass and specific volume HGP, and the difference in the specific volume HGP is much bigger. Thus, from Equation 2, it is evident that the NCM-C battery would go through a higher temperature rise at the same operating

head.ce:title: Comparative study on the heat generation behavior of lithium-ion batteries with different cathode materials using accelerating rate calorimetry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Automotive Technology and Research Center, 68 Xianfeng Road, Tianjin 300300, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China Automotive Technology and Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heat generation behavior is of great significance for the design of both battery cell and thermal management system. In the available literature, there is no report comparing thermal behaviors among different types of lithium ion batteries. In this work, heat generation power of the LFP-C and NCM-C lithium ion battery cells are evaluated using an extended-volume accelerating rate calorimetry. From the map of battery heat generation power, it is found that for the lithium ion battery with certain materials and formulas, there exists an obvious heat generation boundary which is in a power function with the temperature. For the comparison between these two different batteries, experimental results show that the variation tendency of heat generation power between the NCM-C and LFP-C batteries are totally different. In addition, the battery with a NCM cathode exceeds that with a LiFePO4 cathode in specific volume heat generation power.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design optimization of electric vehicle battery cooling plates for thermal performance
Cooperative research on safety fundamentals of lithium batteries
The Limits of Low-Temperature Performance of Li-Ion Cells
Synthesis of pristine and carbon-coated Li4Ti5O12 and their low-temperature electrochemical performance
Factors responsible for impedance rise in high power lithium ion batteries
Measurements of heat generation in prismatic Li-ion batteries
Thermal analysis of large-capacity LiFePO 4, power batteries for electric vehicles[J]
Thermal behavior and electrochemical heat generation in a commercial 40Ah lithium ion pouch cell
Thermal characterizations of a large-format lithium ion cell focused on high current discharges
